Scaling Up Precision Fermentation: Challenges And Strategies
============================================================

![Image 1: Precision Fermentation](https://lee-enterprises.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/precision-fermentation-1024x725.jpg)

LEC Partners

June 5, 2024

Overcoming Obstacles in Scaling Up Precision Fermentation: An in-Depth Look
===========================================================================

In the labyrinth of modern biotechnology, precision fermentation stands as a beacon, casting a transformative glow across the landscape of sustainable food production.

As this emerging technology matures, it brings with it a cornucopia of possibilities, yet scaling up teeters on a tightrope above a chasm of challenges ranging from cost containment to consistency.

Businesses eager to pioneer in this space must dance with complex variables like metabolic pathways and bioreactor design, all while navigating the strict waltz of regulations and consumer acceptance.

To turn the tides and harness the potential of this innovation, one must orchestrate an ensemble of strategies and adapt to the ecosystem’s tempo.

In this article, we will dissect these omnipresent obstacles and articulate pathways of surmounting them, shedding light on the untapped symphony of precision fermentation.

Key Takeaways
-------------

*   Scaling Up Precision Fermentation Involves Navigating Challenges Like High Production Costs, Maintaining Quality, and Regulatory Complexities
*   Innovation in Bioreactor Design and Process Optimization Are Critical for Improving Scalability and Cost-Efficiency in Precision Fermentation
*   Transparency and Consumer Education Are Essential for Market Acceptance of Precision Fermentation Products
*   Sustainable Sourcing and Energy-Efficient Operations Are Integral to the Sustainable Development of the Precision Fermentation Industry
*   Collaborative Partnerships and a Focus on Consumer Trends Are Pivotal for Success in the Competitive Precision Fermentation Market

![Image 2: Precision Fermentation](https://lee-enterprises.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/precision-fermentation-1024x725.jpg)Identifying Key Challenges in Precision Fermentation Scaling
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

As the promethean glow of innovation in precision fermentation illuminates the path to a sustainable future, several formidable barriers rear their heads, casting long shadows over the road to scalability.

Nestled within the very fabric of this technological advancement lie high production costs, a specter that haunts the dreams of even the most seasoned industry titans.

Tied closely to these fiscal phantoms are the Gordian knots of quality maintenance, which seem to tighten with each leap towards commercial volumes.

The semaphore of regulation and market acceptance wavers uncertainly in these shifting winds, sending mixed signals to stakeholders eagerly charting their courses.

Below this surface turmoil simmers the cauldron of technical limitations rooted in antiquated infrastructure, while the search for sustainable raw materials is akin to reaching for a star that twinkles teasingly on the horizon of the bioeconomy.

Addressing these challenges plucks at the very strings of the industry, playing a tune that must harmonize both the practices of today and the possibilities of tomorrow.

### High Production Costs and Scalability Issues

In the arena of precision fermentation, the behemoth hurdle of scaling operations looms large, its shadow shaped by vertiginous production expenses. These intrepid steps towards expansion grapple with the brute force of economic constraints that threaten to outpace the ingenuity of even the most intrepid players, challenging the notion that escalating scale can seamlessly converge with rational budget allocation.

Moving from prototype to widespread adoption, the equation of scalability is further complexified by a matrix of factors, where the cost per unit of bioengineered products still scuffles with the tough might of market competition. Companies are pressed to couple their scientific proficiency with acumen in cost management, a ballet of balance, measuring meticulous attention to quality against the unforgiving scales of affordability.

### Maintaining Quality During Scale-Up

Scaling up precision fermentation, akin to navigating a ship through a tempestuous sea, mandates the preservation of quality’s integrity amidst the tempest of increasing volume. The art of expanding production without diluting the essence of the final product stands tall as a colossus, demanding ingenuity and iron-clad procedures to ensure the genetic blueprint of the microorganisms yields a consistent quality across an ocean of batches.

Amid the journey from laboratory to industry, every flask and bioreactor becomes a crucible for innovation, where the mastery of maintaining sterility and the preciseness of environmental control are the unspoken heroes. As the stakes rise with the scale, the rigor in process execution becomes the sentinel that safeguards the product’s sanctity, with each variable weaving a thread in the rich tapestry of quality that must remain unsullied in face of escalation.

### Regulatory Hurdles and Market Acceptance

The labyrinth of regulatory frameworks presents itself as a maze where every turn demands compliance, with standards as varied as the countries on the map. In the pursuit of market acceptance, these regulatory requirements unfold like an intricate dance between innovation and the status quo, often slowing the zestful pace of scientific and commercial advancements with a shield of caution.

Market acceptance looms as the horizon where the sun of public opinion either rises with warmth or sets in a chilling indifference towards novel foods. Companies entrenched in the domain of precision fermentation must not only navigate the choppy waters of regulatory compliance but also anchor their brand’s trust in the hearts and minds of consumers, transforming uncertainty into the bedrock of a new food ecosystem.

### Technical Limitations in Existing Infrastructure

As companies carve their paths toward large-scale precision fermentation, the pre-existing infrastructure frequently reveals its anachronistic nature: a relic ill-suited for novel demands. The antiquated machinery creaks under the weight of modern needs, throttling the pace at which the industry seeks to race ahead with Darwinian urgency.

| Challenge | Description | Impact on Scale-Up |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Ancient Equipment | Machinery not designed for the specific needs of precision fermentation. | Decreased efficiency, increased risk of contamination and quality inconsistency. |
| Data Management | Inadequate software systems for advanced bioprocess monitoring and data analysis. | Limits dynamic optimization and further scale-up potential. |
| Energy Requirements | High energy-consuming processes not optimized for upscaling. | Expanded carbon footprint and elevated operating costs. |
| Space Constraints | Facilities lack the necessary volume to accommodate scaling production. | A bottleneck that can cap production capacity and hinder growth. |

In an era where data reigns supreme, existing software systems often stumble, unable to juggle the ballooning datasets synonymous with sophisticated fermentation operations. The implications of lagging digital infrastructure echo through the process, as a dearth in robust analysis capabilities stifles innovation, leaving potential breakthroughs floating in the digital ether: unseen and untapped.

### Sourcing Sustainable Raw Materials

In the crucible of precision fermentation, sourcing sustainable raw materials emerges as an alchemical quest: a pursuit to transform base substrates into the gold of nutrient-rich broth without depleting the earth’s bounty. Companies at the forefront of this revolution comb through the mosaic of the planet’s resources, seeking materials that resonate with the ethos of sustainability while ensuring an uninterrupted upstream lifecycle.

This stewardship of nature’s gifts demands a balance between ecological wisdom and industrial needs: a symphony of efficiency where renewable resources harmonize with the rhythm of production cycles. The selection of these materials is not merely a reflection of environmental responsibility but echoes the drumbeat of market viability and consumer acceptance:

*   Assessment of raw materials for minimal environmental impact and maximized regenerative potential, ensuring the viability of precision fermentation processes.
*   Evaluation of the supply chain’s resilience to fluctuations in availability, price, and quality, which influences the steadiness of production scales.
*   Integration of renewable and locally-sourced inputs to minimize carbon footprints and support the tapestry of the local economy.

Innovative Strategies for Cost Reduction
----------------------------------------

In the theater of precision fermentation, attaining the summit of cost-effective production is a pursuit fraught with complexity, yet rich with opportunity.

Sculpting the raw edges of innovation with the chisel of economic pragmatism, industry pioneers are orchestrating a diverse ensemble of strategies to trim the excess from production expenses.

Groundbreaking advancements in bioreactor design are reshaping the landscape, offering a glimpse at machinery tailored for heightened efficiency and reduced expenditure.

Meanwhile, in the ballet of bioengineering, optimizing fermentation mediums and processes becomes a dance of alchemy, transforming common ingredients into elixirs of profitability.

Automation rises as the phoenix of operational efficiency, its wings spreading to cover the tedious expanse of manual oversight.

At the communal table of progress, collaboration with suppliers unfurls as the centerpiece, serving a shared goal of sustainable sourcing that props up both ecological and financial pillars.

These movements in concert form an intricate mosaic, each piece pivotal to the grand vision of scaling precision fermentation without forfeiting fiscal sensibility.

### Advancements in Bioreactor Design

In the crucible of cost optimization, bioreactor innovation emerges as a pivotal frontier, one where the melding of material engineering and computational fluid dynamics paves the way for systems that elevate both yield and cost efficiency. Streamlined bioreactor designs conjure the alchemy of increased production within a minimized footprint, optimizing space and energy use while simultaneously fanning the flames of productivity.

In an industry where precision yields potency, the advent of single-use bioreactors cuts through both the Gordian knot of cross-contamination risk and the tapestry of cleaning costs, thereby granting companies the dual boon of enhanced product purity and reduced downtime. This leap in bioreactor technology weaves itself into the narrative of scalable precision fermentation, contributing a chapter rich with innovation and promise for the burgeoning bioeconomy.

### ![Image 3: Precision Fermentation Scaling](blob:http://localhost/3eb999aae263e1311d321dd13002e9f4)Optimizing Fermentation Mediums and Processes

In the crucible of scaling precision fermentation, the careful optimization of fermentation mediums and processes emerges as a cornerstone of efficiency. The quest for the perfect balance often leads scientists to deploy an arsenal of methods, among them fine-tuning the ratios of nutrients to feed the microscopic laborers: the yeast and bacteria that are the beating heart of bioproduction.

Scribing the intricate recipe for a cost-effective medium is akin to composing a symphony in which every element must be in harmonic proportion: too much of one nutrient and not enough of another can result in a cacophonous discordance that sullies both yield and quality. Thus, the stoic vigilance in monitoring and adjusting these parameters becomes a testament to the artistry woven into the science of precision fermentation.

*   Dive into the genome of the fermentative organisms to understand their nutritional needs and optimize the medium composition.
*   Employ advanced analytics to relentlessly pursue an even-handed economy of scale that respects both nature’s limits and the company’s fiscal sanity.
*   Iterate and refine biochemical pathways within the organisms to enhance their efficiency and output, turning each microbe into a microscopic marvel of production.

### Leveraging Automation for Operational Efficiency

In the chess game of industrial scale biotechnology, automation emerges as the strategic move that could swiftly checkmate inefficiencies. By integrating automated systems across various stages of the precision fermentation process, companies carve a pathway toward operational efficiency: machinery and algorithms harmonize to weave a tapestry of streamlined workflow and augmented speed.

The future of precision fermentation lies in the hands of machines that operate with the grace and precision of an expert artisan, executing tasks with robotic consistency and unwavering accuracy. This orchestration of automated processes reduces human error and elevates productivity to new heights, allowing a seamless flow of production that keeps pace with the heartbeat of demand.

*   Adoption of automated monitoring systems ensures constant oversight of fermentation parameters, adjusting them in real-time for optimal performance.
*   Implementation of robotics in material handling reduces the risk of contamination and accelerates the operational tempo.
*   Utilization of machine learning algorithms fine-tunes production processes, constantly evolving through iteration to create a robust system that learns from every batch.

### Collaborating With Suppliers for Sustainable Sourcing

To fortify the ramparts against high production costs, alliances with suppliers of raw materials become a keystone in the archway to sustainable sourcing. These strategic partnerships are not merely transactions, but collaborations that weave the dual fibers of sustainability and cost-effectiveness into the very fabric of the supply chain.

Forging such alliances allows businesses to architect an ecosystem where both parties prosper from the shared commitment to eco-friendly practices and economic viability:

*   Working closely with suppliers ensures that the raw materials procured for fermentation processes meet the stringent criteria of sustainability without compromising on quality or availability.
*   The co-development of optimized supply strategies results in a resilient meshwork that withstands the ebb and flow of market dynamics, securing a long-term influx of environmentally responsible inputs.

Indeed, these symbiotic relationships serve as a testament to the power of collective effort in the pursuit of a greener future, underscoring the notion that unity in intention and action can mold an industry that honors the earth while it innovates.

Enhancing Quality and Consistency at Scale
------------------------------------------

As the daunting task of amplifying precision fermentation to meet insatiable market demands takes center stage, the industry casts a keen eye on the twin pillars of quality and consistency.

Establishing stringent quality control measures becomes a bulwark against the tempest of variability that often accompanies scale.

Likewise, the adaptation of fermentation techniques to the juggernaut of mass production requires a delicate hand, ensuring that the integrity of each batch remains unscathed by the sheer volume.

Within the crucible of scaling up, harnessing the predictive power of data analytics emerges as a beacon for process optimization, while investment in continuous workforce training solidifies the bedrock of skill, which is paramount for navigating this complex tapestry of growth.

Collectively, these strategies plot a course for a future where the scale of production does not dilute the essence of quality but rather forges a steadfast alliance between the two.

### Implementing Rigorous Quality Control Measures

In the theater of biotechnological advancement, quality control stands as the vigilant guardians of consistency. The geneses of tailor-made quality measures are the result of meticulous evaluation and assessment: a relentless probe into the heart of production processes guarantees that the quality of the product is not an erratic variable but a fixed constant.

The metamorphosis from experimental batches to vast fermentative seas necessitates the sculpting of robust quality control frameworks. Armed with an armamentarium of high precision instruments and protocols, these frameworks ensure that each molecule of the product tells the same story of uncompromised excellence, undercutting the reign of variability with an iron fist of consistency.

| Quality Control Factor | Instrument/Method | Benefit |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Genetic Stability | PCR, Sequencing | Ensures organism’s genome remains unchanged, affecting the product’s quality. |
| Environmental Conditions | Automated Monitoring Systems | Optimizes growth conditions to maintain consistency at scale. |
| Purity Levels | Chromatography, Spectrometry | Detects contaminants that could compromise quality and safety. |
| Process Accuracy | Calibrated Bioreactors | Ensures each production run meets the exact parameters for quality assurance. |

It is the scaffolding of rigorous quality control measures that anchors the lofty aspirations of large-scale manufacturing firmly within the attainable realm. Here, the marriage of advanced technology and a disciplined approach to validation and calibration become the keystones of a fortress, safeguarding the high standards set by precision fermentation.

### Adapting Fermentation Techniques for Mass Production

Mastering the symphony of scaling precision fermentation hinges on the fine tuning of fermentation techniques to suit the grand scale of mass production. This undertaking is akin to the careful orchestration of a complex system, wherein scientists and engineers must collaborate to adapt the intricacies of microbial growth and product formation within expanded production landscapes.

As the volume of production swells, maintaining the fidelity of the fermentation process is paramount—each vessel and bioreactor must become a microcosm of efficiency, maintaining the delicate equilibrium necessary for high-quality production while navigating the increased demands of mass manufacture.

### Utilizing Data Analytics for Process Optimization

In the quest to refine the art of precision fermentation, the power of data analytics emerges as a vanguard, guiding the optimization of processes with meticulous precision. By harnessing this ocean of data, companies can detect patterns and correlations that were once veiled in the depths of complexity, enabling them to fine-tune the fermentation blueprints in pursuit of perfection.

*   Advanced data management systems capture real-time fermentative parameters, translating raw numbers into actionable intelligence.
*   Machine learning algorithms analyze trends to forecast and mitigate potential disruptions, ensuring a stable march towards high-volume production.
*   Data-driven decision-making elevates the process control to near omnipotent oversight, where adjustments are both swift and surgically precise.

Analytic insights blossom into strategies that not only anticipate the needs of the microbial workforce but also preemptively adapt to environmental fluctuations, ensuring that the delicate dance of fermentation remains untroubled by the variables of scaling up: Unleashing a level of command over the process that wields data as both shield and spear in the battle for quality and quantity.

### Continuous Training for Skilled Workforce Development

Within the cathedral of precision fermentation, the continual training of a skilled workforce is a keystone in the arch supporting quality and consistency at grand scales. The intricacies of this craft demand not only a foundational knowledge but an evolving understanding that keeps pace with the cadence of technological and methodological advancements.

The commitment to lifelong learning breathes life into the very heart of sustainability, ensuring that the hands guiding fermentation processes are ever adept and agile: Reflecting one’s knowledge in the sheen of the product illustrates the apex where expertise and high-calibre execution meet:

1.   Deploying comprehensive training programs that encompass both theoretical and hands-on experience, fostering an environment of continuous professional growth.
2.   Establishing mentorship and apprenticeship systems which cultivate practical learning, passing the torch of expertise through the ranks of the corporation.
3.   Investing in certifications and specialized courses that expand the horizons of employees, equipping them with the tools to navigate the frontiers of precision fermentation.

The pursuit of a skilled collective brings forth the artisans of the biological tapestry, whose hands weave the threads of innovation with the meticulous precision of learned maestros. This relentless chase for mastery serves not only to elevate the caliber of the workforce but enshrines a culture of excellence that becomes synonymous with the company’s ethos and output.

Overcoming Regulatory and Market Entry Barriers
-----------------------------------------------

The odyssey of scaling up precision fermentation often finds itself entangled in the web of regulatory complexities and consumer skepticism.

Organizations embarking on this journey must demonstrate dexterity in their ability to not only comprehend but navigate the nuanced tapestry of global regulatory landscapes.

Simultaneously, cultivating trust with consumers necessitates a window of transparency into the practices employed within; as crystal-clear as the proverbial glass through which one peers to gauge purity.

Concurrent to these endeavours is the essential task of wielding the twin instruments of advocacy and educational initiatives, instruments that resonate with the frequency of enlightenment, demystifying the science for the public and stakeholders alike.

Moreover, entering the competitive arena poses its distinct crucible of challenges, compelling enterprises to craft strategies that sculpt the final product’s price point into a figure both appealing and attainable.

These perennial threads, when skillfully interwoven, construct the robust fabric necessary to support the ascendancy of precision fermentation industries through the labyrinthine challenges towards market establishment.

![Image 5: Global Regulatory Landscapes](blob:http://localhost/a9d1801395805f97ffad991230ebf906)

### Navigating Global Regulatory Landscapes

In their quest to harness the science of biotechnology, companies face the Herculean task of satisfying a patchwork of regulations, a journey akin to threading a needle with the sinuous wire of global compliance. The heart of the challenge: each sovereign territory’s regulatory framework, as unique and complex as the genetic code of the organisms at the helm of fermentation, awaits with its own set of demands and expectations.

Understanding and adhering to these varied regulatory landscapes is akin to navigating a star-studded galaxy, where a business must pilot through the constellations of food safety, environmental standards, and biotechnology usage with astute precision and reverence for the laws of each land: With vigilant eyes fixed on this daunting but surmountable expanse, companies strive to chart a prudent and successful course:

| Region | Regulatory Challenge | Strategic Approach |
| --- | --- | --- |
| European Union | Stringent GMO legislation and labeling requirements | Detailed risk assessment and transparent communication of GMO use in products |
| The United States | Complex approval processes involving multiple federal agencies | Engagement with regulatory bodies early in the development process to streamline approvals |
| Asia-Pacific | Varied and evolving regulatory landscapes with diverse cultural perspectives on biotechnology | Local partnership and knowledge exchange to tailor compliance strategy to each market |

These tailored approaches reflect an acute awareness of the global context, a strategic adaptability that turns the challenge of myriad regulations into a dance of compliance: an elegant balance between respecting international customs and nurturing the growth of precision fermentation markets.

### Building Consumer Trust Through Transparency

In the intricate ballet of market engagement, the role of transparency emerges as a pivotal movement, gracing the stage with clarity and fostering deep-seated trust among consumers. By providing a lucid view into the processes behind precision fermentation, companies demystify their craft, sowing the seeds of consumer confidence with truth as their cornerstone.

This steady stream of transparency, flowing freely from the source, empowers consumers to make informed choices, effectively bridging the gap between scientific innovation and public perception. The clear articulation of safety measures, environmental impacts, and the benefits of precision-fermented products serves not only to enlighten but to usher in an era of consumer advocacy, rooted firmly in trust and understanding.

### Engaging in Advocacy and Educational Initiatives

Among the key strategies for scaling up precision fermentation is the deployment of robust advocacy and educational initiatives: a vital undertone in the composition of market entry. Advocacy serves as the clarion call that amplifies the value of precision fermentation technologies, seeking to reshape regulations that might otherwise stifle innovation and growth.

1.   Advocacy efforts focus on influencing policy to pave smoother paths for biotechnology advances.
2.   Educational initiatives aim to elucidate the intricacies and benefits of precision fermentation to the public.
3.   Comprehensive outreach programs work to dispel myths and garner grassroots support for sustainable biotechnological solutions.

Complementing the thrust of advocacy, the curation of educational materials and programs cultivates a groundswell of informed support. Through a concerted effort to enlighten consumers and stakeholders alike, these initiatives lay the groundwork for acceptance and endorsement of cutting-edge fermentation techniques, fostering a fertile landscape for market penetration.

### Formulating Strategies for Competitive Pricing

Within the competitive theater of the market, devising strategies for pricing that strike a harmonious chord between cost-effectiveness and consumer allure becomes an intricate dance. Companies pioneering in precision fermentation must calibrate their pricing tactics to echo the value proposition of their product, enticing discerning customers while maintaining a foothold against the backdrop of a competitive landscape.

Orchestrating this equilibrium involves not just a fine-tuning of production expenses but an astute analysis of the marketplace, setting prices that resonate with the symphony of supply and demand. Abstract
--------

In the era of sustainable development, the use of cell factories to produce various compounds by fermentation has attracted extensive attention; however, industrial fermentation requires not only efficient production strains, but also suitable extracellular conditions and medium components, as well as scaling-up. In this regard, the use of biological models has received much attention, and this review will provide guidance for the rapid selection of biological models. This paper first introduces two mechanistic modeling methods, kinetic modeling and constraint-based modeling (CBM), and generalizes their applications in practice. Next, we review data-driven modeling based on machine learning (ML), and highlight the application scope of different learning algorithms. The combined use of ML and CBM for constructing hybrid models is further discussed. At the end, we also discuss the recent strategies for predicting bioreactor scale-up and culture behavior through a combination of biological models and computational fluid dynamics (CFD) models.

**Keywords:** mechanistic modeling, data-driven, hybrid modeling, scale-up, computational fluid dynamics

1. Introduction
---------------

With the increasing consumption of fossil fuels and related environmental issues, there is an urgent need to find biological substitutes for traditional petrochemical products through green biological manufacturing. Over the decades, microorganisms have been used as ‘mini-factories’ in biomanufacturing to aid with the diversity of metabolic pathways and their accompanying ability to transform a wide range of renewable raw materials into value-added compounds by means of fermentation [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B1-bioengineering-09-00473),[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B2-bioengineering-09-00473),[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B3-bioengineering-09-00473)]; however, the use of wild-type microorganisms in industrial production is usually affected by many factors, such as substrate and product toxicity [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B4-bioengineering-09-00473),[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B5-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Biologists are provided with many modern biotechnologies, such as genetic engineering and synthetic biology, to engineer more powerful mini-factories [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B6-bioengineering-09-00473),[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B7-bioengineering-09-00473),[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B8-bioengineering-09-00473),[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B9-bioengineering-09-00473),[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B10-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Successful examples include _Escherichia coli_, which produce insulin and carotene [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B11-bioengineering-09-00473),[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B12-bioengineering-09-00473)], _Saccharomyces cerevisiae_, which produce geraniol [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B13-bioengineering-09-00473)], _Yarrowia lipolytica_, which produce N-acetylneuraminic acid [[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B14-bioengineering-09-00473)], _Bacillus subtilis_, which produce hyaluronic acid [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B15-bioengineering-09-00473)], and so on; however, the construction of mini-factories is not the end goal of biomanufacturing—biological fermentation and industrial production are the ultimate goal, which is not a simple task. The engineered strains may not perform well in the actual fermentation process due to the lack of suitable fermentation strategies; therefore, optimizing fermentation parameters (such as the medium composition and extracellular conditions) is a key factor in terms of running the mini-factories efficiently, which is crucial for the process of fermentation [[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B16-bioengineering-09-00473),[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B17-bioengineering-09-00473),[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B18-bioengineering-09-00473)]. In practice, the rich insights generated by mathematical modeling can assist in the optimization of fermentation processes [[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B19-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Mathematical models, as approximations of reality, can clearly represent fermentation processes whose intrinsic complexity exceeds intuitive understanding, thus providing indispensable insight into designing, controlling, and optimizing the process, as well as minimizing unnecessary experimentation [[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B20-bioengineering-09-00473),[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B21-bioengineering-09-00473)].

However, it is not easy to model the fermentation process because each cell in the bioreactor can be viewed as a subsystem of metabolic and signaling networks [[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B22-bioengineering-09-00473),[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B23-bioengineering-09-00473)]. For fermentation problems, three modeling approaches are generally used: mechanistic modeling, data-driven modeling, and hybrid modeling [[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B20-bioengineering-09-00473),[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B24-bioengineering-09-00473)]. The mechanistic modeling approaches derive the models from prior knowledge using equations that are notable and acknowledged. Mechanistic models can extract valuable information from the raw data, and they provide insight into the underlying mechanisms [[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B25-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Kinetics and constraint-based modeling (CBM) are the two primary mechanistic approaches for analyzing microbial growth and metabolism [[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B26-bioengineering-09-00473)]. In contrast, the data-driven approaches obtain a model by analyzing and fitting existing data. Data-driven models are also known as black-box models because they cannot provide information about the basic mechanisms without considering their internal structures and phenomena [[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B27-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Machine learning (ML) is a commonly used data-driven approach. With the advancement of omics technology and various analytical techniques, the datasets available for fermentation process modeling are rapidly growing, and academics favor using ML to interpret large-scale datasets for deeper analysis and optimization [[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B28-bioengineering-09-00473)]. As a result of such circumstances, hybrid modeling has come into being, which refers to the integration of mechanistic modeling and data-driven approaches. Recently, comprehensive reviews of hybrid models have been published, indicating that they are a promising prospect for this field [[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B29-bioengineering-09-00473),[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B30-bioengineering-09-00473)].

In addition to the complexity of microbial metabolic behavior, fermentation systems also have complex hierarchical structures. These systems consist of microorganisms and a fermentation environment, and they are influenced by upstream and downstream operating conditions. Moreover, during industrialization, the expansion of the bioreactor volume and changes in shape lead to changes in the fermentation environment, which subsequently leads to the failure of fermentation strategies that are developed during the laboratory stage [[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B31-bioengineering-09-00473)]; therefore, as biological fermentation shifts from laboratory to industrial production, we also need to introduce fermentation environmental changes into biological models to elucidate the effects of mixing and hydrodynamics [[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B32-bioengineering-09-00473),[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B33-bioengineering-09-00473)]. This goal can be achieved by coupling biological models with computational fluid dynamics (CFD) models.

This paper gives an overview of different mathematical modeling methods and their applications in biological fermentation processes. We first introduce the basic forms of mechanistic models that can describe microbial metabolism using kinetic and CBM modeling methods and their applications in biological fermentation processes. Next, we discuss different approaches to building data-driven models using ML. The synergistic effect of CBM and ML is further discussed. In the end, we highlight the coupling of biological models with CFD models, which prompts the formation of model-based integrated tools to successfully predict bioreactor scale-up and culture behavior during model-assisted bioreactor operation design.

2. Methods and Applications of Mechanistic Modeling
---------------------------------------------------

Mechanistic modeling describes some, though not all, mechanisms of complex systems, enabling model parameters to be measured or inferred. Kinetic models are a kind of common mechanistic model, which usually reveal the dynamic changes between metabolites by means of kinetic laws expressed by ordinary differential equations (ODEs) [[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B34-bioengineering-09-00473)]; however, for kinetic models, the mechanism of gene regulation underlying biological phenomena is usually only explained by kinetic parameters in model equations (e.g., enzyme constants and metabolite concentrations), rather than a mechanistic description of gene action [[35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B35-bioengineering-09-00473)], and therefore, kinetic modeling is generally applied to fermentation problems in which the metabolic behavior is well understood. CBM is another mechanistic modeling approach. CBM studies the behavior of genome-wide systems to elucidate the relationship between genotypes, phenotypes, and environmental conditions [[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B27-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Next, we will discuss these two mechanistic modeling methods and their application to fermentation problems in detail.

### 2.1. Application of Kinetic Modeling to Fermentation Processes

The derivation of kinetic models depends mainly upon the level of detail in which microbial growth and metabolic behaviors are described. From a macro perspective, the fermentation process in a bioreactor is a chemical process affected by physical and chemical environmental factors (such as temperature, pH, aeration, and substrate concentration). The transformation of substrate S to product P is catalyzed by bacterial pellets X, which may be seen as a box containing equally disseminated catalysts and chemicals. In this case, we do not need to pay attention to the internal structure of bacterial pellets; we only need to pay attention to the changes in substances and external environmental factors before and after catalysis. Thus, the kinetic models provide a macro perspective on the bioreactor dynamics without specifying compositional and structural details under reasonable assumptions. The kinetic models constructed in this case can also be called unstructured kinetic models. When the substrate concentration (S) is the only limiting element, the Monod model is established. Different unstructured kinetic models of fermentation processes are established with different descriptions of constraints and common kinetic equations, as shown in [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#bioengineering-09-00473-t001). In practice, biologists construct kinetic models by adjusting and combining different equations according to the actual situation. For instance, Zhang et al., established a new kinetic model based on Monod through parameter optimization, which not only described the relationship between products and by-products in the fermentation process of 1, 3-propylene glycol, but it also guided the optimization of the fermentation conditions and improved the yield [[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B36-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Moreover, Garnier et al. developed an analytical solution that combined the Monod model with the Luedeking–Piret equation which can be used to simulate the batch fermentation process [[37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B37-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Although these models address the least complex portrayal of cellular behavior, they can prove particularly valuable when quantifying the phenomenon of growth inhibition during early process development. On the other hand, unstructured kinetic models generally contain few parameters, which, from a computational perspective, is usually advantageous. At present, a significant portion of recent studies concern bioethanol production [[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B38-bioengineering-09-00473),[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B39-bioengineering-09-00473),[40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B40-bioengineering-09-00473)], biosurfactant production [[41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B41-bioengineering-09-00473),[42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B42-bioengineering-09-00473)], bacterial and fungal biomass production [[43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B43-bioengineering-09-00473),[44](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B44-bioengineering-09-00473)], as well as microalgal growth [[45](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B45-bioengineering-09-00473),[46](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B46-bioengineering-09-00473),[47](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B47-bioengineering-09-00473)].

As opposed to unstructured kinetic models, structured kinetic models consider the physiological state of the cell and treat bacterial pellets as multicomponent entities with internal structures. Structure, in structured kinetic models, can be introduced by lumping the metabolites into distinct intracellular pools [[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B25-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Wang et al. constructed a structured kinetic model that can be used to dynamically reflect changes in microalgal biomass by analyzing how carbon is allocated to different intracellular compartments under nitrogen-poor and nitrogen-rich extracellular conditions [[56](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B56-bioengineering-09-00473)]. In a similar manner, Haringa et al. constructed a structured kinetic model describing the growth of _Penicillium_ _chrysogenum_, as well as product formation, by grouping the most important intracellular metabolites into five pools and four intracellular enzyme pools [[57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B57-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Their research also proved that the structured models have a higher accuracy and wider application scope than the unstructured models. We believe that our improved understanding of the behavior of microbial cells, along with advances in various omics techniques, will facilitate the further development of structured kinetic modeling.

### 2.2. Application of Constraint-Based Modeling to Fermentation Processes

CBM is another important mechanistic modeling method that considers the underlying metabolic mechanisms of microorganisms. CBM can be used to analyze the dynamic characteristics of specific target metabolites, and then implement fermentation strategy optimization from the micro-level, which can enhance the diversity and pertinence of optimization target design [[58](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B58-bioengineering-09-00473)]. To build a CBM model, an in-depth understanding of metabolism is first required. Metabolism represents all the biochemical reactions in cells. These reactions form a coordinated set of metabolic pathways that convert substrates into the metabolites required for the physiological activities of the cells at a rate known as metabolic flux. In addition to being a basic determinant of a cellular metabolic state and physiology, metabolic flux is also the key parameter in quantifying metabolic networks [[59](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B59-bioengineering-09-00473)]. CBM plays an important role in the metabolic modeling of quantitative metabolic flux [[60](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B60-bioengineering-09-00473),[61](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B61-bioengineering-09-00473),[62](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B62-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Metabolic flux analysis (MFA) and Flux balance analysis (FBA) are the two primary CBM strategies for quantifying metabolic flux ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#bioengineering-09-00473-f001)), which can be implemented by COnstraint-Based Reconstruction and Analysis (COBRApy) in Python [[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B26-bioengineering-09-00473),[63](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B63-bioengineering-09-00473)].

#### 2.2.1. Flux Balance Analysis

Flux balance analysis (FBA) is a mathematical metabolic flux technique that analyzes the flux of metabolites through a detailed metabolic network (e.g., genomic scale) [[64](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B64-bioengineering-09-00473)]. One of the earliest applications of FBA was used to calculate the given network model and substrate to obtain their maximum theoretical yields of metabolic products ([Table 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#bioengineering-09-00473-t002)) [[64](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B64-bioengineering-09-00473),[65](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B65-bioengineering-09-00473)]. Papoutsakis et al. first demonstrated this in 1984 by describing the interrelationship between various products and biomass in butyrate fermentation using FBA, accurately predicting the theoretical yield of the several fermentation products of butyrate bacteria [[66](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B66-bioengineering-09-00473)]. The same method can be utilized to work out the maximum biomass yield. For instance, Varma and Palsson used FBA to quantitatively anticipate the maximum cell growth rate cell density of wild-type _E. coli_ W3110 [[67](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B67-bioengineering-09-00473)]. In their study, they also used FBA to predict the temporal distribution of glucose as well as the concentration of by-products. Another important application of FBA focuses on the prediction of biomass production under different substrate conditions, which aims to improve medium formulation and/or medium feeding strategies [[63](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9495923/#B63-bioengineering-09-00473)]. # Deep dive: Fermentation upstream bioprocess design

Explore the processes, equipment, and bioreactors critical for commercial fermentation-derived ingredient production.

## Overview of commercial fermentation bioprocesses

Fermentation biotechnology applies engineered biological processes (bioprocesses) using cells, enzymes, or cell components to produce a bioproduct from the metabolism of raw material feedstocks such as carbon and nitrogen. Biomanufacturing already produces chemicals, enzymes, food ingredients, materials, and pharmaceuticals from microbes. Using lessons from the bioindustrial and biopharmaceutical industries, advances in fermentation bioprocess design are enabling the production of food ingredients that can replicate animal-derived ingredients.

Fermentation is a bioprocess that uses microorganisms such as yeast, bacteria, and fungi to convert complex or simple carbon sources into compounds of interest (such as enzymes, metabolites, proteins, vitamins, or oils). Traditionally, fermentation is the microbial conversion of sugar into an organic acid or an alcohol. Food and beverage fermentation conventionally uses microbes to preserve and improve the organoleptic properties of foods like bread, beer, vinegar, yogurt, cheese, and wine ([Paulová, Patáková, and Brányik 2013](https://doi.org/10.1201/b15426)). Now the food industry is developing bioprocesses to create alternative proteins, fats, and other ingredients from microbes through more advanced fermentation processes. Here we describe fermentation in this new context as biomanufacturing bioproducts, including food ingredients, from different microbes and feedstocks beyond classical sugar fermentation to acids and alcohols.

To produce ingredients such as protein-rich mycoprotein biomass or precision fermentation-derived protein, the selected strains and feedstocks are mixed in a bioreactor. Bioreactors, sometimes called fermenters, are where specific biological processes convert feedstocks under controlled conditions. In addition to the essential bioreactor, a commercial fermentation process includes an array of equipment and associated steps from upstream to downstream production (Figure 1). Upstream processes (USP) include all the processing and preparation prior to harvest of the cells, such as substrate/medium preparation, medium sterilization, microorganism growth for inoculation (seed train), equipment sterilization, and finally, growth/production in the main bioreactor. Downstream processes (DSP) include all processing after cell harvest, such as dewatering, extraction, and purification of the fermentation product from the biomass (solid fraction) or supernatant (liquid fraction), along with equipment sterilization and wastewater effluent treatment ([Gutiérrez-Correa and Villena 2010](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/275953938_Characteristics_and_Techniques_of_Fermentation_Systems); [Warner 2019](https://books.google.com/books/about/Industrial_Biotechnology_Commercializati.html?id=3G-CyAEACAAJ)).

Both USP and DSP are crucial to the success and economic viability of the fermentation bioprocess. However, because of the vast options and information in both areas, this deep dive will focus on the typical systems, strategies, and commercial advances in fermentation technology for USP development. A separate deep dive into DSP is warranted as these technologies can often be more complicated and costlier than the fermentation process alone.

![F1. Fermentation usp dsp overview](https://gfi.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/F1.-Fermentation-USP-DSP-Overview-1024x611.png "Deep dive: fermentation upstream bioprocess design 2")

### Bioreactor basics

In USP, microbes and sterile feedstocks are brought together in a controlled bioreactor environment to produce nutrient-rich biomass or a specific metabolite, including enzymes, proteins, oils, or other molecules. Bioreactors can be generally broken into submerged fermentation (liquid state) and solid-state fermentation (SSF) based on the substrate and continuous phase in which the microbe grows. Bioreactor sizes can vary from small benchtop systems (1-20L) at the laboratory scale to large-scale commercial bioreactors (50-1000 m3) for biomanufacturing. Commercial-scale bioreactor volumes are often reported in cubic meters (m3), where 1 m3 equals 1,000 L. Regardless of the scale and type of system, the main function of a bioreactor remains the same: to provide an optimal and controlled environment for the microorganism to produce the product of interest.

The typical inputs into the bioreactor include the media, nutrients, gasses, and the microbe. The main parts of a bioreactor depend on the design and application (submerged vs. SSF), but generally include a vessel, mixing system, inlet/outlet ports (for media, nutrients, inoculum), sterilization system, gas input, gas exhaust, temperature control, and various control systems. Process monitoring and control in a bioreactor involve various sensors and control strategies to optimize the conditions for cell growth, product formation, and yield. Monitoring typically involves measuring critical process parameters such as temperature, gas concentration (primarily dissolved oxygen), mixing rate, pH, humidity (most relevant for SSF), and substrate concentration in real time. This information is then fed into a control system that adjusts process parameters to maintain the desired conditions for optimal microbial growth and production [(](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/XX1D)[Gutiérrez-Correa and Villena 2010](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/275953938_Characteristics_and_Techniques_of_Fermentation_Systems)[)](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/XX1D).

Any heat generated in the process must be removed from the system to maintain ideal growth temperatures for the microorganism. Temperature control is crucial to bioreactor operation as the temperature can greatly affect the growth rate, metabolism, and product formation. The temperature control system typically consists of a temperature sensor, a heating and cooling system, and a controller [(H. J. Noorman et al. 2018)](http://dx.doi.org/10.1039/9781788010320-00001). The heating and cooling system generally includes an integrated jacket, internal coils, or an external heat exchanger where the circulated water is cooled by a chiller or heated by a steam heat exchanger. The temperature sensor measures the current temperature inside the bioreactor, and the heating and cooling system adjusts the temperature as needed to maintain the desired set point [(](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/6Ivy)[H. J. Noorman et al. 2018](http://dx.doi.org/10.1039/9781788010320-00001)).

Gas delivery and control in a bioreactor provide key substrate gasses and ensure optimal gas mixing and heat transfer necessary for cell growth and production. Gas delivery, such as air, oxygen, and carbon dioxide, is regulated by airflow controllers like mass flow controllers or blowers to ensure flow rate control. The gas delivery system mixes the gasses in the desired proportions and introduces the air to the vessel. In submerged liquid fermentation processes, gas is delivered through a sparger, a submerged air diffuser found at the bottom of the vessel. Gas flow is typically expressed as air delivery volume per volume of liquid per minute (VVM) to standardize the gas flow rate across different vessel sizes [(Zhang et al. 2019)](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bej.2019.107307). In SSF, gas ventilation serves the same function in gas exchange and can be delivered through gas diffusers and blowers. Air ventilation in SSF also has a strong role in providing adequate heat exchange in the bioreactor [(Ge, Vasco-Correa, and Li 2017)](https://doi.org/10.1016/B978-0-444-63663-8.00013-6). For both submerged and SSF, gas control is often monitored through in-line sensors like dissolved oxygen probes and exhaust gas monitors.

Each fermentation system type has different bioreactor components and controls based on the system needs. For instance, submerged fermentation systems must maintain controlled pH ranges for the microbe through inline probes and acid or base addition. For SSF systems, humidity monitoring and control are more highly prioritized considerations.

## Types of upstream fermentation technologies

### Submerged and solid-state fermentation

Many different bioreactors can be classified in several ways, such as by gas transfer, agitation type, water content, feedstock, and temperature.  In general, bioreactors can be classified as either submerged or SSF systems. In submerged fermentation, the substrate to be fermented is always in a liquid phase along with the nutrients required for microorganism growth, and gas exchange occurs via mixing. By contrast, in SSF, the microorganism grows on a predominantly solid substrate with gas delivery through aeration and a reduced liquid phase ([Sun, Shahrajabian, and Lin 2022](https://doi.org/10.3390/fermentation8120688)). There can be technology overlaps between the two fermentation systems, though, such as with [liquid-air interface fermentation](https://patents.google.com/patent/US11261420B2). See Table 1 for key features and differences in submerged and SSF systems.

Submerged fermentation, the most common industrial fermentation process, enables a tightly controlled environment for optimal production with diverse microorganisms and feedstocks (Table 1). ([Paulová, Patáková, and Brányik 2013](https://doi.org/10.1201/b15426)). This includes bacteria, microalgae, yeast, and other fungi across many metabolism types.

Beer production is an example of a traditional submerged fermentation process in which fermented sugars are converted to alcohol under anaerobic and often unmixed conditions. The majority of precision fermentation products, like enzymes, are produced through submerged fermentation because of the tight, reproducible control needed over the system. However, the energy costs, water usage, and feedstock costs can be much higher in submerged than in SSF processes.

| **Submerged** | **Solid-state** |
| --- | --- |
| Water is main component of culture | Reduced or no free-flowing water |
| 2-phase system: liquid – gas Liquid is the primary phase for temperature control and gas exchange. Gas is delivered through the liquid phase for mixing and gas transfer. | 3-phase system: solid – gas – liquid  Gas is the primary phase for temperature control and gas exchange.  Gas is delivered across solid and/or liquid interfaces for heat and gas transfer. |
| Homogenous system; good mixing | Heterogeneous system; poor mixing |
| Dissolved nutrients in water; lower average concentration of nutrients | Complex insoluble substrate; higher average concentration of nutrients in feedstock |
| High feedstock material cost (usually); Able to utilize gas feedstocks | Lower feedstock material cost; Able to utilize different agricultural sidestreams |
| Applicable to a wide range of microorganisms | Limited range of microorganisms used |
| Microorganisms grown in solution; Absorb nutrients from the liquid medium; Little to no nutrient gradient when mixed. | Microorganisms adsorb to or penetrate substrate surface (typically);  Absorb nutrients from the solid substrate; Nutrient gradient exists |
| High energy usage | Lower energy usage |
| Good process control and monitoring  (e.g., temperature, pH, DO, nutrients) | Reduced and poor process control and monitoring |
| Better heat and mass transfer | Limited heat, nutrient, and gas transfer |
| Final harvest is a liquid with lower product concentrations | Final harvest is a wet substrate with high product concentrations |
| Robust DSP options | Difficult to separate substrate from the final product |

The other type of bioreactor fermentation used in the food industry is solid-state fermentation (SSF), also called solid substrate fermentation. SSF is a fermentation process where the microorganisms are cultivated on moist, solid, non-soluble organic material with little to no free water. The solid substrate, such as grains, seeds, legumes, husks, peels, or other organic materials, acts as a support and nutrient source for microorganism growth ([Yafetto 2022](http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e09173)). SSF originated in traditional food fermentation to produce foods like bread, tempeh, miso, and koji. For example, tempeh results from steamed soybean fermentation by the fungus *Rhizopus,* whilekoji is the product of cooked rice fermentation by the fungus *Aspergillus.*

The industry has seen an increased interest in SSF for producing many bioproducts, including enzymes, organic acids, mushrooms, single-cell proteins, secondary metabolites, and more. The microorganisms used are typically fungi, which are suited for SSF because their hyphae can grow into the solid substrate, penetrating the inter-particle space and digesting cellulosic material [(](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/7lx1)[Yafetto 2022](http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e09173)[)](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/7lx1). SSF can be a valuable food processing approach for bioconverting organic [agro-industrial wastes](https://gfi.org/resource/cultivating-alternative-proteins-from-commodity-crop-sidestreams/) like husks, straw, and peels, which cannot be as readily implemented in submerged fermentation, into bioproducts. Fungi are predominantly used in SSF to produce enzymes, citric acid, fermented animal feed, single-cell protein, and mycoprotein. Only some yeast and bacteria are used in solid-state fermentation to produce organic acids like acetic acid ([Sun, Shahrajabian, and Lin 2022](https://doi.org/10.3390/fermentation8120688)). In the alternative protein space, SSF holds great promise for creating whole-cut meat alternatives and improving plant-based feedstocks’ organoleptic and nutritional properties. There is untapped potential for SSF use in precision fermentation to reduce commercial costs.

The primary advantages of SSF are the simpler design, lower capital cost, reduced contamination risk, lower water usage, lower energy usage, and the usage of underutilized feedstocks. However, SSF is limited by substrate heterogeneity, slower organism growth, feedstock sterilization, and limited gas and heat transfer rate (Table 1). In addition, imprecise process control over parameters like temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen, and nutrient availability is a key limitation of SSF ([Paulová, Patáková, and Brányik 2013](https://doi.org/10.1201/b15426)). SSF is also partially limited by the types of microorganisms that thrive in low-moisture environments ([Sun, Shahrajabian, and Lin 2022](https://doi.org/10.3390/fermentation8120688)).

It is difficult to cover the diverse bioreactors used for microbial fermentation comprehensively. Below are the typical bioreactor types used in the alternative protein sector, though, there is always room for new bioreactor designs or the application of other bioreactors to the alt protein sector.

## Submerged fermentation bioreactors

A typical submerged fermentation bioreactor is an upright, closed, cylindrical stainless steel tank, such as a stirred tank bioreactor (STR) or an airlift bioreactor. The basic features typically include a headspace volume, agitation system, gas delivery system, gas exhaust port, temperature control, acid/base inlet, sampling port(s), cleaning fluid port, drain port, medium, and nutrient delivery ports, and aseptic inoculum port [(](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/XX1D)[Gutiérrez-Correa and Villena 2010](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/275953938_Characteristics_and_Techniques_of_Fermentation_Systems)[)](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/XX1D). All submerged fermentation bioreactor design is dictated by liquid mixing, gas mass transfer, and heat transfer.

Mixing affects cell homogeneity, heat transfer, and cell access to feedstock, nutrients, and oxygen. The mixing and gas delivery impacts mass transfer of gasses from the bubbles to the cells via the liquid phase [(](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/6Ivy)[H. J. Noorman et al. 2018](http://dx.doi.org/10.1039/9781788010320-00001)[)](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/6Ivy). Gasses are delivered via spargers in most reactors, but the mixing and distribution depend on the bioreactor design. Temperature control in submerged fermentation reactors generally includes either an integrated jacket, internal coils, or an external heat exchanger. Cooling is accomplished by chiller water heat exchangers or via the integrated jacket with circulating chiller water. Steam heat exchangers typically provide heating. The method for cooling and heating depends on the bioreactor type and scale. Process control in a submerged fermentation reactor is tightly controlled through mixing, temperature exchange, gas exchange, nutrient dosing, and pH regulation [(](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/XX1D)[Gutiérrez-Correa and Villena 2010](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/275953938_Characteristics_and_Techniques_of_Fermentation_Systems)[)](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/XX1D).

Most commercial submerged processes are aerobic, with the microorganism requiring oxygen (i.e., aerobic heterotrophs). Thus, adequate gas-liquid exchange is required for growth. For aerobic fermentation, the extent of gas-liquid exchange is characterized by the oxygen transfer rate (OTR; mmol L-1 hr-1) and the volumetric mass transfer coefficient (kLa), which details how efficiently oxygen is transferred from the gas phase to the liquid medium. Gas mass transfer is greatly affected by pressure, temperature, vessel geometry, medium viscosity, gas bubble size, gas delivery rate, and agitation or mixing [(](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/XX1D)[Gutiérrez-Correa and Villena 2010](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/275953938_Characteristics_and_Techniques_of_Fermentation_Systems)[)](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/XX1D). For instance, smaller bubbles increase the surface area for gas diffusion, and higher temperatures reduce gas solubility, while higher pressures increase gas solubility ([Henry’s Law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henry%27s_law)). For other microorganisms, gas exchange of feedstocks like methane, hydrogen, carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, and other gasses is critical for growth. For example, industrial processes with methanotrophs, hydrogen oxidizing bacteria, and phototrophic microalgae rely on gas feedstock fermentation. The mass transfer rate of any gas feedstock is an important control parameter and a primary design consideration in submerged fermentation bioreactors.

Below are the typical submerged fermentation vessels used commercially (Figure 2):

### Stirred-tank bioreactor (STR)

A stirred tank reactor (STR) is the most common type of submerged bioreactor. These reactors are closed cylindrical tanks equipped with mechanical agitation systems consisting of an external motor drive, agitator shaft, impeller for radial or axial flow, and baffles on the tank sides to increase fluid turbulence and prevent a vortex [(](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/6Ivy)[H. J. Noorman et al. 2018](http://dx.doi.org/10.1039/9781788010320-00001)[)](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/6Ivy). Gasses are delivered through a perforated sparger at the bottom of the tank. Stirred agitation allows for improved gas dissolution and distribution along with microorganism and nutrient distribution throughout the bioreactor to ensure a uniform environment [(Gutiérrez-Correa and Villena 2010)](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/XX1D). STRs offer good mixing for high viscosity medium due to the agitator shear mixing, which can be beneficial for high productivity precision fermentations with yeast, for example. However, the high mixing can cause foaming, which can negatively impact gas venting and equipment. Moreover, high shear may not be desirable if the microbe is shear-sensitive or if a specific cell morphology must be maintained, such as with fungal mycelial or filamentous biomass. STRs are often used at laboratory-to-pilot scale from 0.25-100L for research purposes, process optimization, or small-scale production. Commercially, STRs have widespread usage with volumes up to 150-200 m3 but are generally more expensive to build and operate than smaller systems. For example, in even larger STR (such as 500 m3), sufficient mixing requires an immense energy input increase, which often makes it economically unviable compared to bubble columns and airlift reactors [(](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/Ykuq)[Warner 2019](https://books.google.com/books/about/Industrial_Biotechnology_Commercializati.html?id=3G-CyAEACAAJ), [H. J. Noorman et al. 2018](http://dx.doi.org/10.1039/9781788010320-00001)[)](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/6Ivy).

### Bubble column bioreactor (BC)

Bubble column (BC) reactors are attractive for their simple, cost-effective design and lower energy costs. In a BC reactor, gas is sparged through a column containing the liquid medium and microorganisms, and mixing takes place due to the upward movement of gasses through this column [(](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/XX1D)[Gutiérrez-Correa and Villena 2010](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/275953938_Characteristics_and_Techniques_of_Fermentation_Systems)[)](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/XX1D). The sparger nozzle at the bottom of the reactor must be evenly distributed to ensure homogenous flow and mixing. Sieve plates or columns can be added to facilitate mixing and dispersion [(](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/XX1D)[Gutiérrez-Correa and Villena 2010](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/275953938_Characteristics_and_Techniques_of_Fermentation_Systems)[)](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/XX1D). A disadvantage of BC reactors is reduced liquid circulation between upper and lower portions, which can be overcome by a recirculating pump or through an airlift bioreactor. BC reactors can be run up to a 1000 m3 capacity, such as in citric acid production with *Aspergillus niger* ([Paulová, Patáková, and Brányik 2013](https://doi.org/10.1201/b15426)). The production of bakers’ yeast (*S. cerevisiae)* is one of the oldest and most common bioprocesses and usually takes place in large aerobic BC fermenters (100-300 m3) using molasses as a feedstock in a fed-batch process [(](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/7SSu)[H. J. Noorman et al. 2018](http://dx.doi.org/10.1039/9781788010320-00001)[)](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/6Ivy).

### Air-lift bioreactor (ALR)

An air-lift bioreactor (ALR) is a modified version of a BC reactor with a separated loop whereby gas drives the liquid culture up a riser, and then the liquid culture falls back down through a downcomer, improving liquid mixing over a BC reactor. The loop can be internal to the system using baffles or barriers or an external loop where the degassed liquid returns through a separate downcomer. The pressure difference between the riser and the downcomer aids in fluid circulation ([Merchuk and Gluz 2002](http://dx.doi.org/10.1002/0471250589.ebt029)). There are many variations of the ALR using the loop principal to mix the bioreactor. For example, Quorn uses an external loop ALR driven by an airlift riser to grow its *Fusarium venenatum* mycoprotein ([Moore, Robson, and Trinci 2019](http://www.davidmoore.org.uk/21st_century_guidebook_to_fungi_platinum/Ch17_18.htm)). ALRs are often used at large-scale volumes, such as Quorn’s 150 m3 ALR, as they allow for well-mixed gasses, reduced cell shear, lower power consumption, and reduced capital cost.

### Gas or loop bioreactor

U-loop bioreactors are gas-liquid contactor vessels often used in gas fermentation and are similar to external ALRs in terms of liquid flow path. However, gas is not the driving mixing force in the U-loop bioreactor. A pump system facilitates mixing to circulate the liquid. The gas is introduced at the top of the reactor downcomer tube or introduced elsewhere along the flow path, where the liquid phase is recirculated downward faster than the gas bubble rise rate. This facilitates very efficient gas-liquid mass transfer [(Olsen et al. 2020)](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Sten-Jorgensen/publication/267316574_Modeling_Simulation_and_Optimization_of_Single-Cell_Protein_Production_in_a_U-Loop_Reactor/links/5e637d96299bf1744f660022/Modeling-Simulation-and-Optimization-of-Single-Cell-Protein-Production-in-a-U-Loop-Reactor.pdf). These reactors are used in gas fermentation with methane and other gaseous carbon sources to produce single-cell proteins and biofuels. Commercial examples include Unibio and Calysta, producing single-cell protein through methanotrophic gas fermentation.

### Photobioreactor (PBR)

A photobioreactor (PBR) is used to cultivate photosynthetic microalgae and cyanobacteria, which utilize light as an energy source and carbon dioxide as a carbon source. The general design optimizes cost, light utilization, and carbon dioxide delivery (gas mass transfer). PBRs can take many forms as either a closed system or an open system. Open photobioreactors are typically raceway ponds, which are mixed by a paddle wheel, open to the environment, and relatively inexpensive to build and operate [(](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/XX1D)[Gutiérrez-Correa and Villena 2010](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/275953938_Characteristics_and_Techniques_of_Fermentation_Systems)[)](https://paperpile.com/c/AAxtUJ/XX1D). Closed photobioreactors are characterized by a transparent tubular vessel or panel array that is optimized for light delivery outdoors or when outfitted with a light source. Closed systems can include modified BCs, airlift PBRs, tubular plug flow reactors, and more. These systems provide more control, higher productivity, and contamination prevention but often come at a higher capital and operation cost. Thus, PBR systems often produce high-value, low-volume products like nutraceuticals, cosmetics, and pharmaceuticals [(Araújo et al. 2021)](https://doi.org/10.3389/fmars.2020.626389). In food, PBR systems are utilized for the production of *Arthrospira* (Spirulina) biomass for its nutrition and ease of production, while the carotenoid astaxanthin from *Haematococcus* has been an attractive antioxidant ingredient due to its high functionality and market value at over $1,000 per kg ([Stachowiak and Szulc 2021](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8125449/#:~:text=The%20cost%20of%20production%20of,is%20higher%20than%202000%20dollars.)).

![Examples of submerged fermentation bioreactors.](https://gfi.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/F2.-SmF-bioreactors-1024x660.png "Deep dive: fermentation upstream bioprocess design 3")

## Solid-state fermentation bioreactors

The many advantages of solid-state fermentation (SSF) suggest it has abundant economic potential. However, its industrial applications are not as diverse as for submerged fermentation, and a key limitation is a lack of simple and practical automated fermenters for SSF processes. Since the predominant phases in SSF are gas and solid, the system is not as homogeneous for monitoring and control, with the potential for poor mixing and heat removal (Table 1) [(Chen 2013)](http://dx.doi.org/10.1007/978-94-007-6043-1). Successful SSF application relies heavily on bioreactor design and precise operation due to the heterogeneous mixture and poor heat and gas mass transfer.

Traditionally, SSF bioreactors can be classified into static and dynamic systems based on their mixing. These can also be classified as stationary, intermittent, or continuous stirred processes with ventilation as either natural convection or forced air ([Chen 2013)](http://dx.doi.org/10.1007/978-94-007-6043-1). Both agitation and ventilation have a strong impact on temperature control and mass transfer. Gas ventilation is a primary mechanism to drive temperature and humidity or moisture control in SSF bioreactor systems, though some SSF reactors use jacketed cooling systems for heat removal [(Chen 2013)](http://dx.doi.org/10.1007/978-94-007-6043-1).   
The four predominant types of SSF bioreactors (Figure 3) are (1) reactors without mixing and forced aeration (tray reactors), (2) reactors without mixing but with forced aeration (packed-bed bioreactors), (3) reactors with mixing but no forced aeration (stirred drum bioreactors), and (4) reactors with mixing and forced aeration (stirred-aerated bioreactor) ([Ge, Vasco-Correa, and Li 2017](http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/B978-0-444-63663-8.00013-6)).

### Tray bioreactor

Using trays is the most common and oldest system due to their very simple design. Tray reactors are used in static conditions with no mechanical agitation — these include traditionally unmixed beds with no forced aeration on the solid substrate [(Manan and Webb 2017)](http://dx.doi.org/10.15406/jabb.2017.04.00094). Tray bioreactors consist of a chamber containing stacked trays, where the air is circulated around the trays to facilitate gas exchange and temperature and humidity control [(Couto and Sanromán 2006)](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jfoodeng.2005.05.022). Trays can be outfitted with perforated mesh bottoms to facilitate airflow through the substrate or as a solid tray where the air is only ventilated over the tray top. Heat exchange and gas mass transfer through the beds rely only on diffusion since there is no mixing and no forced air blown through the substrate beds. Thus, the tray only holds a limited amount of solid substrate to facilitate exchange. It is a simple design that has been traditionally used in tempeh and koji production ([Ge, Vasco-Correa, and Li 2017](http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/B978-0-444-63663-8.00013-6)). enriched_points.expanded_content: High production costs, quality maintenance, regulatory complexities, and outdated infrastructure hinder efficient scale-up in precision fermentation, risking product consistency and market acceptance.
Innovative bioreactor designs, automation, data-driven modeling, and strategic partnerships enhance scalability, cost-effectiveness, and quality control, ensuring successful fermentation processes.

Understanding these challenges and strategies is crucial for optimizing future fermentation projects, promoting sustainable practices, and enhancing market competitiveness in the evolving biotechnology landscape.
